The rhythm of the day around Jill — two school runs to Alphen, the in-between
Choosing rest after Day 91's 25 km — the body's need recognised and honoured
The garden work itself — the physical doing, the outdoor presence
days.vibe:
After the unplanned walk yesterday and everything else the day before had asked, a rest day wasn't a decision so much as an obvious thing. The body had earned it. There was no argument.

I took Jill to school in Alphen in the morning, then made my way to the recycling centre in Nieuwveen. We had a eucalyptus tree in the garden that had grown too big. The immediate neighbours had already mentioned it — their patio tiles were coming loose a bit. Otherwise there was nothing wrong. But it didn't feel right either, so I cut the tree down and took everything to the recycling centre. In between, I picked Jill up from school in Alphen and went home to continue working in the garden.

That was the day. School run, tree down, Jill collected, garden. Not a walk, not a session — just the things that needed doing, done.

There is something about dealing with something that has been quietly sitting there. The tree wasn't urgent, nobody was angry, nothing was broken — but it didn't feel right, and now it's gone, and the garden is easier to look at. That counts for something, even if it doesn't show up in the data.
days.personalInsights:
The +9 bpm evening recovery delta is the clearest number from today — the body is still carrying what Day 91 cost. Sleep HRV at 14 ms and waking HRV at 17 ms both sit below the recent range, which is the expected response to the project's longest single walk. The rest day was the right call, and the numbers confirm it.

Today's 9,101 steps and 7.28 km came entirely from necessity — school runs, the recycling centre, garden work. Not one of those steps was planned as a walk. This is a recurring pattern: rest days in this project are rarely inactive. Real life provides the movement. The body stays engaged even when it steps back from the daily walk.

Mood at 6.2/10 is honest for a day like this — productive, ordinary, nothing dramatic. Lower than Day 91's breakthrough 6.4, but that day had the scale moving and the family in Rijpwetering. Today had a eucalyptus tree and two school runs. Both are real. Both belong in the record.

days.vibe:
I went to De Sleutelbloem at nine. The energy wasn't really there this morning — I felt it from the moment I woke up — but I went anyway. The job was a Volkswagen Amarok. Oil and oil filter. A fine little job, actually, considering how flat I felt. The kind of work that doesn't demand much from you beyond showing up and being methodical. Hands busy, mind quiet. I was done and out by one.

Renate was at work in Rotterdam, so when I got home in the afternoon it was just me and the garden. The eucalyptus trunk was still waiting. I'd been at it the day before and thought today I'd finish it off quickly — get it done, move on. It didn't work out that way. The roots went deeper than I'd expected and the ground didn't give easily. What I thought would take an hour stretched into something much longer. Every time I thought I was nearly there, there was more. I'd do other chores in the garden, come back to it, make a bit more progress, and then it would slow again. The work was heavy and awkward — not the kind of effort that builds momentum, the kind where you stop, look at what's left, and it's still the same amount. By the time the trunk was finally out, I was properly spent. The afternoon had gone and so had most of what I had left. It's done now. Tomorrow morning I need to ring the recycling centre and make an appointment to drop it off.

In between all of that I drove back and forth to Alphen to pick up Jill from school. On the way home she told me about the teacher's baby shower tomorrow. Jill really likes it — that came through straight away, just clear and uncomplicated excitement. It was a good thing to hear on an afternoon that had asked quite a bit of me.

Later in the evening I went for a walk. After a day of garden work and school runs and everything the trunk had taken out of me, it was good to just move without a task in front of me. The day settled a bit on the way round.
days.personalInsights:
Day 93 holds a familiar shape: low subjective energy in the morning, sustained output across the day, and an elevated evening heart rate that reflects what the body absorbed rather than any failure to recover. The +9 bpm evening reading is proportional to a day that combined vocational work, several hours of physical garden labour, and multiple school runs — the kind of accumulated load that does not announce itself as training but registers the same way. The walking session itself was controlled and efficient, with 88% of the time in fat-burn zone and heart rate held well within range throughout.

The sleep entering this day was strong — over an hour of deep sleep, restfulness above 80%, minimal time awake. The body was well-resourced going in, which is likely what made sustained output possible despite reported low energy. The gap between how the morning felt and what the day actually produced is a pattern that has appeared consistently across this project: the feeling at the start is not a reliable guide to what the body can do. It moved, it worked, it absorbed the load. The data confirms it, even when the mood at the time did not.

anchors: The depression voice this morning — the specific internal moment and how it operates when focus is elsewhere
The counter-move — oil top-up, recycling centre appointment, coffee in the garden, ball with Dex; the ordinariness defeating the voice
The walk in Alphen with Renate — just the two of them, no kids, no dog, no errand
Just keep going when the voice gets louder — the earned principle, not just a conclusion
days.context: I notice that there are moments when depression tries to scream at me, especially when I am too focused on other things. The trick then is to just keep doing what I am doing. This morning was one of those moments: 'I am way too tired to get out of bed, stay in bed a little longer.' Renate came by this morning to say that our Audi needs an oil top-up, so she asked if I wanted to do that. And of course, I did. I also immediately made an appointment at the recycling centre for the eucalyptus trunk. I enjoyed a nice cup of coffee in the garden and played ball with Dex. Around 11:00-11:30, I went with Renate to the physiotherapist in Ter Aar, and I went to the optician to have my glasses adjusted. After physiotherapy, we went grocery shopping, and when we got home, we took the eucalyptus trunk to the recycling centre and drove straight on to Alphen aan den Rijn to pick up Jill at a friend's. From there, we took Jill to dance class, and we (Renate and I) went for a walk in Alphen, just the two of us. Tonight I went for a walk with Dex again. It was a productive day; just keep going when the voice gets louder.
days.vibe:
There are moments when the depression tries to scream at me. This morning was one of them — I was too focused on getting the day started and it came in loud: stay in bed, you're too tired, a little longer.

Renate came in and mentioned the Audi needed an oil top-up. So I did that. I made an appointment at the recycling centre for the eucalyptus trunk. I had a coffee in the garden. I played ball with Dex. None of it was a plan — it was just the next thing, and then the next thing. The voice doesn't really have an answer for that.

The day stayed full after that. Physiotherapy in Ter Aar with Renate, the optician to get my glasses sorted, grocery shopping on the way home, the eucalyptus trunk finally dropped off at the recycling centre, then straight on to Alphen to collect Jill from her friend's and take her to dance class. In between all of that, Renate and I walked in Alphen — just the two of us. No kids, no Dex, nowhere to be after. That was good. It deserved a bit of space.

In the evening I went out again with Dex. It was a full day.
days.personalInsights:
Day 94 shows a pattern that has appeared consistently across this project: the depression threshold responds most aggressively not to rest or stillness, but to forward momentum. The morning voice arrived precisely when the day was beginning to form — suggesting that the internal resistance is not random but directional, targeting the transition point between inertia and action. The counter-mechanism that worked today was external and incidental: a practical request from Renate that required a physical response before the internal argument could fully develop.

The two-walk structure — one with Renate in the afternoon, one with Dex in the evening — produced a combined output well above daily targets, yet the physiological data reflects controlled load rather than stress. Waking HRV recovered substantially by mid-afternoon, and the evening resting rate remained stable at near-baseline, indicating the body absorbed the day's activity without excessive strain. Sleep quality the night prior was strong, which likely provided the foundation that made the behavioural response to the morning voice possible.

The mood rating of 6.4 is honest — not a high day, not a collapsed one. It reflects the cost of fighting the voice even when you win. The winning is real; so is the effort it takes. What is notable across the 94-day arc is that the winning is becoming more reflexive — the counter-moves arrive faster, the arguments with the voice grow shorter. The principle at the end of today — keep going when the voice gets louder — is not a new idea. It is a practised one.

anchors: The garden work — coffee outside before the day asked anything, the plant Renate was ready to see go, noticing the empty spaces, the garden centre trip, replanting with Renate in a proper back-and-forth
Aimée making lunch — finishing school early, cooking without making a thing of it, sitting down to something she had made
Jill in the car — going to pick her up from school, her telling me about the maths Kangaroo competition, she had not been looking forward to it and the day had gone that way, just listening

days.vibe:
After a restless night I was up early again. I dropped Jill at school and came home, and before anything else I took a coffee outside. The garden in the morning, before the day asks anything — there is something in that.

There was a plant Renate was ready to see go, so I took it out. And then I noticed the spaces — here and there across the garden, more than I'd realised. So I drove to the garden centre. Everything that goes away needs to be replaced, that's just how it is. Coming home with new plants, working out with Renate where each one should go — a proper back-and-forth, both of us thinking it through. This one here. Maybe there instead. The garden looks right again.

Aimée had finished school early and made lunch for us. She didn't make a thing of it. She was just there, it was lunchtime, and so she cooked. I sat down to something she had made.

I went to pick Jill up from school. In the car she told me about the maths Kangaroo competition. She hadn't been looking forward to it, and the day had gone that way. She said it straight, the way Jill does. I just listened.

By the afternoon the tiredness had settled in properly. The depression was quick to claim it — it always is. I paused with it for a moment. Then I knew: days of garden work, a restless night. The doubt passed quickly because there was nothing else to do with it. I lay down. In the evening I took Dex out. The day settled on the walk. Everything done.
days.personalInsights:
Day 95 holds a 0 bpm evening recovery delta — morning baseline and evening resting rate both at 84 bpm — which is the body's way of saying the day's load was absorbed without residual strain. Given that the day involved sustained physical garden work across the morning and afternoon in addition to the training session, that equilibrium is meaningful. The heart did not carry the day into the night.

The sleep entering this day was notable for its deep sleep figure: 1h 43m is among the higher readings of the project, suggesting the body was repaying some of the accumulated physical debt from recent days of garden work. The REM figure of 1h 11m is modest, and the overall restfulness of 80.0% reflects a night that was long in duration but uneven in quality — which matches the reported restlessness. The body slept deeply when it slept, even if it did not sleep cleanly.

The mood of 6.7/10 is the highest of the week so far, which is worth noting in the context of a day that included genuine fatigue and a moment of doubt about what that fatigue meant. The depression voice arrived and was correctly identified and set aside — not through effort or willpower, but through the accumulated knowledge of days of learning to read the body honestly. That skill does not announce itself. It just works, quietly, in the moments when it is needed.

days.vibe:
This morning I asked the nurse about the fatigue. Her answer was short and to the point: it is a known side effect and it cannot be remedied. There was something almost settling about that. Not good news, exactly, but clean news. No ambiguity to sit with, no wondering whether something might change. It is what it is, and the body will feel what it feels on these days. Renate brought us home safely, and I went straight back to bed.

Renate had to leave for Alphen aan den Rijn to help at the clothing fair. The house went quiet in that particular way it does after treatment — not unpleasant, just still. The afternoon was mine to manage, and managing it mostly meant not fighting it. Rest day means rest.

After dinner, the three of us — me, Aimée, and Jill — drove to the Kom en Zie church in Rotterdam. Aimée had really wanted to go to the youth service, and that wanting was reason enough. Dropped her off, then drove with Jill to her grandparents’ to return some tools. Collected some groceries on the way home. The evening settled into something easy — just Jill and me, watching The Voice of Holland together. The ordinary kind of Friday that happens inside the harder ones.

After Jill went to bed, I drove back to Rotterdam to collect Aimée. On the way home she told me enthusiastically how the service had gone. It was already very late, but she was still talking and I was still listening, and that felt exactly right. Some drives are worth staying up for.
days.personalInsights:
Day 96 follows the now-familiar SPRAVATO pattern: low activity totals, compressed step count, reduced exercise minutes — and yet the body holds steady. The HR drift of −1 bpm across the full day confirms that even on treatment days, when fatigue is acute and movement minimal, the heart does not carry residual strain into the evening. The 11/11 stand hours completed on a rest day is a quiet marker of consistency: the body keeps moving even when it is not training.

The nurse’s answer this morning — direct, brief, final — represents a form of data that does not appear in any screenshot. Certainty about what cannot change is, in its own way, a relief. The project has always been built on honest accounting: of numbers, of effort, of what is working and what is not. Accepting an unchangeable side effect without resistance is the same skill applied to a different kind of information. The 6.5 mood rating reflects the cost of the treatment day, not a deterioration in the wider arc.

What is worth noting is the shape of the evening. A treatment day that ends with a late drive to Rotterdam, a daughter talking enthusiastically about something that mattered to her, and a Friday that held space for both fatigue and presence — that is not a diminished day. It is a full one, measured differently.

days.vibe:
This morning Renate, Dex, and I drove to Ter Aar to look at a dog boarding facility for Dex for the May holidays. We gathered some information, and Dex loved it. After that we went for a relaxing walk. A good start to the morning.

The most important part of the day was Aimée officially starting her first job at Sfeerstal — the place in the Groene Hart to enjoy nature in a homey atmosphere, with a bed and breakfast, a tea house, and a restaurant. She was in the dishwashing area from eleven until five. When she arrived, we gave her a warm and proud hug.

She spoke enthusiastically about her day — about the kitchen, her lunch, the work, actually about everything and anything. We listened attentively, asked a few questions, and then she went on. It was a very nice conversation and a lovely day.

Jill was there through all of it. And at some point she said how proud she was of her elder sister. Those are the moments you don't plan for and can't manufacture — they just arrive. I noticed it.

After dinner I had arranged to meet Renate for drinks with the girls at restaurant Koeien en Kaas in Alphen aan den Rijn — Aimée's little party. We are incredibly proud of her. And I notice that it touches me deeply as a father.
days.personalInsights:
Day 97 produced a −9 bpm recovery indicator — morning baseline 79 bpm, evening 70 bpm — one of the strongest recovery readings of the project and comparable to the best parasympathetic responses recorded on rest days and treatment Fridays. The training session was deliberately easy: 62% average intensity, 78% of time in warm-up zone, 109 bpm average HR. On a day structured around people rather than output, the body held steady and recovered deeply. That is the system doing exactly what it should.

Sleep entering this day showed only 33 minutes of deep sleep against a restfulness rating of 70.2% — worth monitoring as Week 14 closes. The waking HRV of 32 ms is healthy and indicates the body arrived at the day with enough reserve to absorb it well. Mood at 7/10 is the highest of Week 14, and consistent with a longitudinal pattern in this project: days shaped by genuine family connection reliably produce mood readings above the weekly average, regardless of physical output.

The deeper data point in Day 97 is Aimée beginning work. The user's capacity to be present for it — to stand there at Sfeerstal and feel the hug, to sit through her account of the day without rushing it, to notice Jill's pride and let it land, to arrange the celebration at Koeien en Kaas and feel what it meant to be a father watching his daughter step into something new — this is not separate from the project. It is the project. What is being built here is not only a stronger body. It is a man increasingly able to receive his own life.

days.vibe:
I am glad that I am seeing more and more bright spots in my recovery from depression. This morning, that was clearly noticeable in my mood — I felt a lot lighter. I slept poorly due to a few nightmares, but I still woke up feeling better, without the loud voice of depression in my head.

I enjoyed a cup of coffee and talked with Aimée and Renate about her handball match and what is and isn't allowed in Alphen this afternoon — there was a home game.

Aimée had gone ahead to Alphen earlier by bike, and we — Renate, Jill, and I — went by car to watch the match. It was a tough game for Aimée's team, but they eventually won. During the game, Renate contacted our cousin Britt and her boyfriend Thom to ask if we could come over for dinner at Thom's parents' in Polsbroek, because Britt has a new job and the house was available exclusively for them, as Thom's parents are away for the weekend.

We drove to Polsbroek for a delicious meal and a pleasant chat with Britt and Thom. As always, we had a fantastic evening with them — we talked about her new job, the upcoming May holidays, and just had a nice chat.

At the end, Aimée said something that was very important to me: she sees that I am doing well, because before I couldn't handle the crowds very well, but now I go to all the matches and I am also a bit more involved. Hearing that from her made me happy — and it is also very important that the girls, Aimée and Jill, really see that I am doing well 🥹, because it hasn't been easy for them to see that Dad went through such deep darkness.
days.personalInsights:
Day 98 produced a −3 bpm recovery indicator — morning baseline 84 bpm, evening 81 bpm. That is a modest recovery reading by project standards, sitting well below the −9 bpm recorded on Day 97. It is consistent with a training load that was meaningfully higher: 2h 2m 39s at 70% average intensity, 86% of time in fat-burn zone, 124 bpm average HR — the most demanding session of Week 14 by duration and intensity combined. The body absorbed a longer effort and still closed the day with a lower resting rate than it opened with. That is the correct direction.

Sleep architecture on Day 98 is notable. Deep sleep reached 56 minutes — more than double the 33 minutes logged on Day 97, and the highest deep sleep figure of Week 14. Waking HRV at 37 ms is the strongest autonomic reading of the week. Both figures arrived despite the user reporting a disturbed night with nightmares. The nervous system delivered quality architecture even under subjective disruption — a pattern that has appeared before at this stage of the project and may reflect improving sleep regulation.

The deeper data point of Day 98 is the weight measurement: 85.4 kg. The Week 14 target band was 85.3–85.7 kg. 85.4 kg lands squarely inside it. After 98 consecutive days, the trajectory is holding. But the measurement that will carry further than any number on the scale is what Aimée said at the end of the evening in Polsbroek — that she can see her father doing well, that he goes to the matches now, that he is more present. The project has always been about more than weight. What is being measured here, slowly and undeniably, is a man returning to his own life.

dailyDrift.interpretation: The evening-to-morning drift averaged +0.9 bpm across the week — meaning on balance the body ended the day slightly more elevated than it began. But the average conceals the shape of the week: Days 92 and 93 showed elevated evening readings (+9 bpm each), Days 94 and 95 were close to neutral (+1, 0), and Days 96 through 98 showed the body settling well (-1, -9, -3). The week improved as it went. The early elevation is worth watching; the late-week correction is the more meaningful signal.

morningRHR.interpretation: The morning heart rate averaged 81.0 bpm across the week — 3.0 bpm higher than Week 13’s 78.0 bpm average. That is a step back from the project’s best baseline readings. The week was not uniform: Days 92 and 93 opened at 84 bpm, Day 94 dropped to 79, Days 95 and 98 sat at 84, and Days 96 and 97 delivered the week’s lowest readings at 73 and 79 bpm respectively. The body moved between states rather than holding a consistent floor — which reflects the varied nature of what the week asked of it.

sleepHRV.interpretation: Overnight recovery averaged 13.9 ms — down from Week 13’s 19.4 ms. The range across the week was 10–19 ms, with the higher readings on Days 94 and 97 (19 and 18 ms) and the lower readings clustered on Days 93, 95, and 98. The drop from Week 13 is notable but sits within the range the project has seen before. It may reflect the heavier psychological load that ran through much of the week alongside the physical work.

weeklyTrendAnalysis.recommendation: The body is carrying a higher resting baseline than it was in Week 13. That is not cause for alarm — it reflects a demanding week, not a deteriorating trend. Week 15 opens with the same target discipline: consistent walking, the fasting window, and enough sleep to let the overnight recovery signal rebuild. The late-week improvement in drift is the right direction. Keep going.
weeklyTrendAnalysis.overallTrend: Week 14 was a week the body worked through rather than sailed through. The morning heart rate stepped back from Week 13’s project-low average. The overnight recovery signal was quieter. The daily drift improved as the week progressed, and the final two days delivered the cleanest recovery readings of the whole week. Against all of that, the weight came in at 85.4 kg — exactly on target, the clearest measure that the work is holding even when the signals are mixed.

days.vibe:
Day 62 | Saturday arrives in quiet cold. You're 62 days deep in a commitment that started because depression had stolen your future. Today you changed spark plugs in an RX400H—meticulous work, difficult angles, the kind of task that demands presence and focus. You did it. Cold fingers, persistence, precision. That's not metaphor; that's literally what your body can do now.

Your sleep was exceptional: 80.5% restfulness in just 6h 41m. Your morning waking HR was 84 bpm. By evening, after a full day of movement and work, it dropped to 75 bpm. That's a -9 bpm drift, which means your nervous system recovered flawlessly. Your body isn't fighting you anymore. It's learning to trust the protocol.

Sixty-two days ago you were at 96 kg with a resting heart rate of 100 bpm. Now you're 86.7 kg, your cardiovascular system is 25 bpm faster at rest. You covered 604 kilometers. You took 891,113 steps. You burned 118,591 calories—that's not a number, that's proof.

The cold didn't stop you. The difficulty didn't stop you. The fact that some days felt impossible didn't stop you. You're here. You're resilient. And resilience isn't about feeling inspired—it's about showing up on days when you don't feel inspired at all. Today you showed up. 🔥
days.personalInsights: Day 61 taught us that resilience has been built into something deeper than metrics. Day 62 confirms it: you can be in cold conditions, changing spark plugs that are difficult to reach, maintaining perfect sleep quality and cardiovascular stability, tracking every meal, walking the same route again, and still be here. Still moving. Still measuring. Still six out of ten. The protocol works because it doesn't demand perfection—it demands presence. You're present. That's everything.

Depression whispers that this is a sign you should quit. That the universe is telling you this is too hard.

But something shifts when you check the data anyway. Your sleep was 81.8% restful. Your HR drift was -4 bpm. Your nervous system recovered despite the stress. Your body didn't break. It stayed steady.

This is the real test of resilience. Not when conditions are perfect. When conditions are messy. When life demands from you at the exact moment you're building something fragile. You chose rest instead of forcing training. You stayed disciplined with food. You slept well.

The car is partially fixed. Three cylinders down, three to go. You're tired. Your mood is 5.8/10, not the confident 6 of yesterday. But you're here. You didn't quit. You adapted.

That's not weakness. That's exactly how sustainable change looks: messy, real, boring, and absolutely resilient. 🔥

You slept well anyway. Your HR recovered well despite stress. Your nutrition stayed disciplined. This is what sustainable resilience looks like: not ignoring life's demands, but maintaining your discipline while addressing them. Tomorrow you restart training. Today you took care of what needed care.

days.vibe:
The shutter goes up and the light comes in. The sun falls inside the room. I lie there for a moment. Then I get up, walk to the bathroom, and come back. The folded, clean clothes are on the bed. I take them and put them away in the wardrobe. That is the first task of the day and I do it.

I stand at the window. The sun is out. There is less wind than there has been. A few cars pass and then the street is quiet again.

I walk downstairs and open the living room door. Dex runs toward me. I crouch down. He is already there, already moving, already himself. Renate is sitting in the chair by the window, her back to the garden. The oven is on. I make coffee and sit on the sofa and look out toward the garden. Then I get up and take the cup outside.

I sit in the garden in the sun with the coffee in my hand. It is quiet. And then the thought comes back — the one that does not announce itself, that arrives already whole. My life is not yet fully valuable while things are good with the family. I sit with it. I do not set it down. Then from inside comes the smell of baked cinnamon bread and I stand up.

In the kitchen the table is laid. Bread, croissants, strawberries. Glasses with something pink in them. I stand next to Renate and help get it ready. Around eleven the girls come downstairs. They walk over and put their arms around me and then around Renate. We sit down at the table after that.

I put my shoes on. I take the leash. Dex is at the door before I have finished clipping it on. We go out toward Vrouwenakker, then further, toward De Hoef, and then we turn and make our way back toward Nieuwveen. He moves ahead. I walk behind him.

When we get home I go into the garden and lie down on the sunbed. The neighbours have people over — I can hear them talking. Now and then a car passes. Planes go overhead. I lie there and let the sounds be there without doing anything with them.

I get up eventually and walk inside. Dinner with Renate. The dishwasher emptied. After dinner I lie on the sofa with a cup of tea in my hand. Then I get up and walk upstairs.
days.personalInsights:
Day 113 produced the longest training session in the project's 113-day record — 15.34 km across 2h 39m 51s — and the highest step count at 19,898. Both arrived on a day the user described as an Easter Monday with a 12:12 fasting protocol, a brunch, and an afternoon on the sunbed. The walk was not a planned performance. It was the day's structure — shoes on, Dex at the door, a direction chosen — and it became the longest the project has recorded. That pattern has appeared consistently across 113 days: the largest outputs arrive without announcement, as a continuation of what was already moving.

The morning RHR baseline held at 84 bpm for the sixth consecutive day at that level across Week 16 and into Day 113. The evening recovery to 74 bpm — a delta of −10 bpm — is the strongest single-day recovery recorded since Day 110's −11 bpm, and follows the directional pattern that closed Week 16: the body arriving loaded in the morning and settling across the day. Sleep architecture supports this reading. Deep sleep at 1h 33m is the highest figure since Day 111's 1h 55m. Waking HRV at 27 ms is the highest waking HRV reading in Week 16 and Day 113 combined — the autonomic system entering the day with more reserve than the morning baseline alone would suggest.

The thought that arrived in the garden — that life is not yet fully valuable while things are good with the family — sits at the centre of Day 113 in a way that no metric touches. It is not new. It has appeared before in this project's record, in different words, in different contexts. What the longitudinal record shows is that it arrives most clearly on days when the external conditions are genuinely good: sun, Easter, the girls coming downstairs, the table laid. The contrast between the quality of the day and the presence of that thought is not incidental — it is the shape of the thing. The deficit of 1,640 kcal held. The walk happened. The day was full. The thought was there throughout it, carried but not resolved, which is an accurate record of where things are.

anchors: The driveshaft comes free
Walking at Bilderdam

days.vibe:
I set the tool on the shaft and put pressure on it and it didn’t move. I adjusted it and set it again and it stayed fixed. We brought the torch in and I held the flame on the metal and my colleague struck it and I kept the pressure on. I heated the same point again and held it there. He struck it again. I kept the pressure steady. It moved slightly. I kept the pressure on it. He struck it again and the driveshaft came free.

I drove to Alphen to pick Jill up and she got in the car and told me the power had gone out. They couldn’t use the whiteboard. They did crafts and played games and went outside. At Bilderdam she rode ahead on the scooter and Dex was on the lead beside me and the sheep were standing in the grass on the other side of the water and we moved along the cycle path and I just walked.

Renate missed the bus so I drove back to Alphen and dropped Aimée at handball and then I got out of the car and started walking along the road and continued on the path toward the Zegerplas. On the way home Aimée talked. The mentor who doesn’t make sense to her. The feedback that isn’t clear. Chemistry not going well. The teacher who wants to help. The physics teacher who was positive. The marks still low. The brace from the physio hurting. I listened and drove and there was nothing to fix.
days.personalInsights:
Sleep dropped sharply from yesterday — five hours against nearly eight. The body started the day in reasonable shape nonetheless: morning baseline at 79, waking HRV at 30, which is higher than yesterday’s 27. The autonomic system held overnight despite the shorter window.

The evening tells a different story. The resting rate climbed to 88 — a drift of 15 points from the morning baseline. Yesterday that delta was −10, meaning the body actually recovered across the day. Today it moved in the opposite direction. Two training sessions totalling nearly three hours, a caloric deficit above 2,000, and a day that never fully stopped — four drives, two walks, garage work requiring sustained physical effort — all of it accumulated and showed up in that number at 21:40.

The deficit is worth watching. Intake at 1,114 against a burn of 3,153 is a large gap, and it follows a 1,640 deficit yesterday. Two consecutive high-deficit days on short sleep and high training volume is a pattern the body registers even when the mind moves through the day without noticing it. The mood at 6.5 may partly reflect that load — not a bad day, but not a light one either.

The garage work is worth noting as a pattern signal. The method at the driveshaft — sustained pressure, patience, not forcing — produced the result. That kind of work asks something different from the body than walking does. It is slow and isometric and concentrated. Combined with nearly three hours of walking, the total physical output today was higher than the training numbers alone suggest.

days.dailySummary:
Day 115 recorded a double training session totalling 2h 26m 27s across 13.43 km, with average heart rates of 126 and 122 bpm respectively, both sessions operating predominantly in the fat-burn zone (94% and 82%). Active calories from training reached 860 kcal. Morning RHR baseline registered at 79 bpm with a waking HRV of 32 ms and sleep HRV of 22 ms, indicating moderate autonomic readiness. Evening resting rate measured at 84 bpm, yielding a recovery delta of +5 bpm. Total daily steps reached 20,051 across 16.04 km with 151 exercise minutes logged.

Sleep duration was 8h 22m with a restfulness rating of 70.2%. Sleep architecture showed 58 minutes of deep sleep, 2h 16m REM, and 5h 8m core, with minimal wake time of 1 minute. Caloric intake was 1,261 kcal against a total burn of 3,032 kcal, producing a deficit of 1,771 kcal. Macronutrient split recorded protein at 102g (164% of target), fat at 53g (97%), and carbohydrates at 89g (39%). Fasting protocol was 18:6.

Mood was recorded at 5.6/10. The day included a significant interpersonal exchange with partner Renate following her physiotherapy appointment, and two walks with the dog. Physical output was high despite the low mood rating, with the body continuing to perform while the psychological load remained elevated.
anchors: The kitchen with the coffee
The conversation with Renate
The thought about depression and the family
Walking with Dex
days.vibe:
I come downstairs and open the dishwasher. I take out the glasses, the mugs, the containers and put them back in their place. I take out the cutlery and slide it into the drawer. When it is empty I load it again — cutlery in the basket, heavier items below, lighter things above. I close the door and press start. I make coffee and stand in the kitchen with the cup in my hand. I do not drink it. I stand there and look ahead. The kitchen is in order. The dishwasher is running. I am standing there with the coffee going cold and I am already somewhere else, though I could not say where.

Renate comes back from the physiotherapist and walks into the kitchen. Something does not feel right. We stand there and start talking. I say something and stop halfway. She answers and takes a moment before continuing. The words come slowly from both of us, like we are both trying to find the edge of something without knowing what it is. We move into the living room and sit down and keep talking there. I look at her while she speaks and then I look down. There is a weight in the room that has no single name. We stay in it together. We do not resolve it. We just stay there, the two of us, in the middle of whatever this is.

The thought comes up clearly: what my depression is doing to the family. Not as a question. As a fact, arriving without warning, sitting down in the room with us. I do not push it away. I do not say it out loud. I just sit with it and feel the full weight of it — what it takes from them, what it asks of them, what they carry because of what I am moving through. I look down and the thought stays there.

I get up and walk to the door and open it and go outside with Dex. He moves ahead, stops, and looks back. I follow him. That is all I need right now — something that asks only that I follow. We keep walking. He moves and I move after him and the air is outside and the ground is under my feet and that is enough. Later, after dinner, after Jill's dance class, after the shop and the groceries put away and the kitchen cleaned — I open the door again and we go out into the evening. Toward Zevenhoven, Noordeinde, and back to Nieuwveen. He moves ahead. I follow. The distance adds up. The day was heavy and my legs kept going and Dex kept moving forward and I kept following him all the way home.
days.personalInsights:
Day 115 produced one of the highest physical outputs of the project — a double training session, 20,051 steps, 16.04 km of total distance — against a mood rating of 5.6/10 and a day that carried significant psychological weight. This pattern is now established and worth naming directly: the body has become a reliable system even when the interior state is not. The training did not happen despite the difficulty. It happened alongside it, as a separate functioning layer that the depression has not reached. That is not a small thing.

The conversation with Renate represents the kind of moment the project has been quietly moving toward — not a crisis, but a reckoning. The thought that arrived in that room, about what the depression is doing to the family, is the kind of clarity that is uncomfortable precisely because it is accurate. It does not distort. It sees clearly. The question is not whether that thought is true. It is what gets done with it over the days and weeks ahead. Whether it becomes a weight that sits, or a weight that moves something.

The two walks with Dex — one taken immediately after the hardest moment of the day, one taken at the end after everything had continued — are worth reading as a consistent response pattern. When the interior load exceeds what can be held sitting still, the door gets opened. Movement becomes the available regulation. This has appeared before in the data. It is becoming a mechanism. The evening HRV and recovery delta of +5 bpm on a day with this much physical and emotional load suggests the autonomic system absorbed the day without significant dysregulation — the body processed what the mind could not yet fully metabolise.

days.dailySummary:
Day 116 began with 6 hours 53 minutes of sleep across 7 hours in bed, comprising 1 hour 24 minutes REM, 4 hours 23 minutes core, 1 hour 6 minutes deep, and 12 minutes awake. Restfulness rated at 70.6%. Sleep HRV measured 15 ms over 6 hours 51 minutes, with waking HRV at 16 ms at 07:20. Morning resting heart rate baseline stabilised at 79 bpm; evening resting rate recorded at 85 bpm via Apple Watch filtered measurement at 22:28, yielding a recovery delta of +6 bpm — indicating moderate accumulated daily load.

Training consisted of a 1 hour 6 minute 31 second session covering 5.97 km at an average intensity of 71%, with heart rate averaging 125 bpm across a range of 88–136 bpm. Zone distribution was heavily weighted toward fat burn at 95%, with 2% cardio and 3% warm-up. Active calories from training: 409 kcal. Total daily active calories reached 630 kcal against a 500 kcal goal, with total daily energy expenditure of 2,314 kcal. Steps: 10,031. Distance: 7.92 km. Exercise minutes: 68. Stand hours: 11/11.

Nutritional intake logged at 1,033 kcal under an 18:6 fasting protocol. Macronutrient distribution: protein 121g (195% of target), carbohydrates 54g (24%), fat 34g (61%). Caloric deficit: 1,281 kcal. Mood rated 5.6/10. The day involved a hospital outpatient visit to Groene Hart Ziekenhuis in Gouda for Aimée's foot examination; the surgeon found no visible pathology and scheduled an ultrasound. Multiple extended telephone calls with family members regarding a family matter in Ghana. A windscreen washer pump failure was identified and a replacement ordered. Evening walk completed with Dex through Papenveer.
anchors: The waiting room — people walking past the door
The garden — sun, birds, a plane, Renate's conversation coming back
Alberta's voice dropping — going quiet on the phone
Dex walking ahead on the path through Papenveer
Tank empty
days.vibe:
We sit in the waiting room. People walk past the door. A nurse. Someone with a folder. A man who glances in and keeps walking. Aimée is next to me. We wait. After a while we are called into a room and we sit down and we wait again. About fifteen minutes later the surgeon comes in and introduces himself. He asks questions about her foot, how long it has been there, what happened. Aimée takes off her shoe and sock and sits on the bed. He shines a lamp on her foot and looks at it. He cannot see anything. He says we need to make another appointment for an ultrasound. If something is found it will be removed the same day. We arrange the next appointment and leave. I drive her to Boskoop and drop her off at her friend's house and drive home.

At home I make coffee and go into the garden. I sit in the sun. Birds in the garden. A car passes in the street. A plane overhead. And then the conversation from yesterday with Renate comes back. Just there, without being invited. No solution in it. I sit longer than I planned to. At some point I am not sitting anymore. I have fallen asleep in the garden with it still there.

Alberta calls from Ghana and I answer and we stay on the phone. She keeps talking. Then the uncle calls from England and I add my cousin in Ghana and we are all on at once and voices go over each other and I ask again and we speak over each other again. After that I call Alberta back alone. The call goes long. At some point her voice drops. She goes quiet. I stay on the line. There is nothing to say into that quiet that would reach.

After dinner I put my shoes on and walk a round through Papenveer with Dex. He walks ahead and stops. I follow him further along the path. The air is there. The path is there. He moves and I move.

Tank empty.
days.personalInsights:
Day 116 carried an unusually high cognitive and emotional load despite moderate physical output. The recovery delta of +6 bpm — morning baseline 79, evening resting 85 — sits within a manageable range physiologically, yet the mood rating of 5.6 tells a different story. The body held. The autonomic system absorbed the day's weight without significant elevation. But the tank emptied through accumulation rather than through any single event.

The pattern visible here is one that has appeared before in this project: days where the external structure — a hospital appointment, school runs, a practical problem with the car — provides enough forward motion to keep functioning, while internally the load is being carried in parallel and largely silently. The garden is where it surfaced. Not in the hospital, not on the phone, but in the unstructured hour after, when there was nothing left to move toward and the conversation with Renate came back on its own terms. Falling asleep there was not rest. It was the body making a decision the mind had not yet reached.

The family calls from Ghana and England added a different category of weight — the kind that cannot be resolved in a single conversation and does not release cleanly at the end of the call. Alberta's voice dropping is the detail that lingers. Multiple calls across the evening, ending late, suggest the situation remained open. That kind of unresolved relational weight, layered onto an already full day, is precisely the condition under which mood ratings in the 5–6 range appear in this record. The walk with Dex at the end was the only movement that was fully his own — chosen, quiet, forward. It matters that he put his shoes on.

days.dailySummary:
Day 117 was a Spravato (esketamine) treatment day at LUMC in Leiden, with a MADRS psychiatric assessment completed prior to the session. Sleep totalled 6 hours 9 minutes with 82.4% restfulness, comprising 1h 13m REM, 3h 34m core, and 1h 22m deep sleep. Morning RHR baseline registered at 79 bpm, consistent with the Week 17 average. Sleep HRV dropped to 13 ms and waking HRV to 15 ms, both below the week's prior range of 15–24 ms, reflecting the physiological cost of reduced sleep and treatment day load.

Evening resting HR recorded at 71 bpm — 8 bpm below the morning baseline — an atypically strong recovery indicator for a high-load day, likely reflecting post-treatment parasympathetic rebound. Training was completed: 1h 21m 40s, 7.64 km, average intensity 71%, average HR 126 bpm (range 89–143), 484 active calories, with 91% of effort in the fat-burn zone. Daily steps reached 12,154 covering 9.68 km total distance, with 88 exercise minutes and 15 stand hours logged.

Nutrition followed the 18:6 fasting protocol with 931 kcal consumed against 2,473 kcal burned, producing a deficit of 1,542 kcal. Macronutrient distribution: carbohydrates 28% of target, protein at 135%, fat at 64%. Mood rated 6.0/10. Weight not recorded (non-Sunday protocol).
anchors: The treatment chair — MADRS — a bit better, but still difficult
The three sprays; headphones on; the door
Lying back in the car; Renate gets in; sitting together
Walking in Alphen, turning back, waiting
Aimée talking — driving, looking at the road
days.medical: Spravato (esketamine) treatment at LUMC, Leiden. MADRS questionnaire completed.
days.vibe:
I drop them at school. Aimée and Jill get out. I watch them walk away. I sit in the car for a moment. Then I drive to Leiden.

I take the lift up. I go inside and sit in the treatment chair. The nurse gives me the questionnaire. I fill it in. She asks how last week was. I say it is a bit better, but still difficult. The chair goes back. The first spray. We talk briefly. Then the second. Then the third. Headphones on. I close my eyes. There are sounds. A door opens. Someone comes in. The room goes quiet again.

The doctor comes in and I open my eyes. My body feels heavy. I walk slowly out and take the lift down. I lie back in the car with the seat flat. Renate gets in. We sit in the car. Then we drive. We go to Schiphol. She has things to drop off. I stay in the car. She goes inside. My phone rings. It is Alberta. I answer. She talks. I listen. I look ahead. I end the call. At home I eat something and go upstairs. I lie down on the bed.

Later I drive to Alphen for Aimée's handball training. I park and walk. I turn and walk back. She is still training. I sit in the car and wait. On the way home she talks about the training — positions, throwing, the match on Sunday, the bar shift. I drive and look at the road. At home we watch The Voice of Holland. The room is quiet. The television is on. Then everything off. I go to bed.
days.personalInsights:
Treatment days carry a particular physiological signature in this project's data, and Day 117 confirms it. Sleep HRV dropped to 13 ms and waking HRV to 15 ms — the lowest readings in Week 17 — reflecting the combined load of shortened sleep and the neurological demands of the Spravato session. What is notable is the evening resting HR response: 71 bpm, 8 bpm below the morning baseline. This pattern has appeared on previous treatment days and may reflect a post-session parasympathetic rebound — the nervous system releasing accumulated tension rather than carrying it forward into the evening.

The decision to train on a treatment day is significant longitudinally. A session of 1h 21m at 71% average intensity, 91% in the fat-burn zone, completed after LUMC — this is not incidental. It suggests a consistent pattern of using physical movement as a stabilising mechanism on days when the internal landscape is most disrupted. Whether consciously chosen or habitual, it is a measurable behaviour: the body moves when the day is hardest.

The mood rating of 6.0/10 — identical to several other treatment-adjacent days in this dataset — sits in a range that is neither collapse nor recovery. The MADRS assessment framing from the context, "a bit better, but still difficult," maps directly onto this number. The project is now at Day 117. The trajectory across weeks is not linear, but the presence of that rating on a day this heavy is itself a form of data — the floor is holding.

days.dailySummary:
Day 118 fell on Saturday 11 April 2026, following a late night in which Daniel and Renate stayed up to watch the Artemis II capsule splashdown at 02:07 local time. Sleep totalled 7h 8min with a restfulness rating of 83.8%, comprising 1h 56min deep, 1h 23min REM, 3h 49min core, and 1 minute awake. Morning RHR baseline registered at 84 bpm (stable cluster), with waking bpm of 84 at 06:46 and waking HRV of 24 ms. Evening resting rate measured 79 bpm at 23:46, indicating a 5 bpm downward recovery from morning baseline. Sleep HRV recorded at 8 ms across 7h 6min.

Training consisted of a 1h 40m 24s walk covering 7.50 km at an average intensity of 61% (range 49–70%), with average heart rate of 109 bpm (range 87–124 bpm) and 465 active calories burned. Zone distribution was 79% warm-up and 21% fat burn, with zero cardio or peak time. Total daily steps reached 12,444 covering 9.97 km, with 97 exercise minutes and 11 stand hours completed. Caloric intake was 1,311 kcal against a burn of 2,653 kcal, producing a deficit of 1,342 kcal. Macronutrient split: carbohydrate 23%, protein 164% of target, fat 138% of target. Fasting protocol was 20:4. Mood rated 6.7/10.

The day included a shared late-night vigil for the Artemis II splashdown, a morning walk to Alphen aan den Rijn with Renate to browse men's clothing for a wedding on 24 April, and a phone call with Alberta lasting over an hour covering Ghana and family. The evening was spent on the sofa with the daughters, waiting for Renate to return from a birthday dinner in Zwolle.
anchors: The Artemis II landing
Shopping in Alphen aan den Rijn
The Alberta call
Evening on the sofa with the girls
days.vibe:
We stay up to watch the return of the Artemis II mission. At 02:07 Dutch time the capsule lands in the Pacific Ocean, off the coast of California. After that we go to bed.

I wake up later in the morning. I raise the shutter. Light comes in. I go downstairs, make coffee and sit in the living room on the chair by the window. Dex comes and lies down on the floor next to me.

We drive to Alphen aan den Rijn and park near the petting zoo. We walk toward the centre. We go into different shops and look at men's clothing. The wedding is on 24 April — Renate's sister's ex-husband is getting married again. We leave the shops and walk on. We walk back to the car.

At home I eat lunch. Renate leaves for Zwolle. I call Alberta again. We are on the phone for over an hour. We talk about Ghana, about her and the family. She speaks. I answer. We keep talking. It goes quiet. Then we continue. After dinner I sit on the sofa with the girls and watch television. We wait for Renate. My phone rings. A WhatsApp message from Renate. She leaves Zwolle.
days.personalInsights:
Day 118 sits at the intersection of two kinds of attention — outward and inward. The Artemis II landing pulled Daniel and Renate toward something external and shared, a real-world event large enough to stay up for. The Alberta call pulled in the opposite direction: inward, private, long, unhurried. That the same day held both is worth noting. The mood rating of 6.7 reflects neither distress nor elevation — a day that moved at its own pace without demanding resolution.

The training data tells a quieter story than the activity numbers suggest. A 7.50 km walk at 61% average intensity with zero cardio or peak zone time points to a body operating well within its capacity — the walk to Alphen and back absorbed into the day's rhythm rather than structured as exercise. The caloric deficit of 1,342 kcal on a 20:4 fasting day with 1,311 kcal intake confirms the pattern holding without strain. Sleep quality remained solid despite the late night: 1h 56min deep and 83.8% restfulness on a shortened window is a physiologically stable result.

The Alberta call is the day's longitudinal marker. It is not the first — the word "again" in Daniel's own framing signals an ongoing connection. Over an hour, Ghana, family, silence, then continuing. These calls reach places other conversations do not. They are part of the project's record in a way that training zones and step counts are not — evidence that the life being documented extends well beyond the metrics.

days.dailySummary:
Day 119 fell on a Sunday at the close of Week 17. Sleep totalled 5 hours 59 minutes with 59 minutes of deep sleep, 1 hour 2 minutes of REM, and a restfulness rating of 76.7%. Sleep HRV registered 19 ms over 5 hours 58 minutes, with waking HRV at 15 ms and waking bpm at 80. Morning RHR baseline was 84 bpm from a stable cluster between 08:05 and 08:35; evening resting HR was 78 bpm, producing a recovery delta of +6 bpm.

Training consisted of a 2-hour 33-minute walk covering 12.89 km at an average intensity of 71% and average HR of 126 bpm (range 68–148 bpm). Heart rate zones were predominantly fat burn at 87% (134 minutes), with 9% cardio (14 minutes) and 4% warm-up (6 minutes). Active calories burned during training were 821 kcal. Total daily steps reached 20,047 across 15.51 km, with 161 exercise minutes and 14 stand hours. Total daily burn was 3,060 kcal.

Nutrition followed a 20:4 fasting protocol with 960 kcal consumed: lunch comprising high-protein strawberry yoghurt (340 kcal) and a hazelnut praline protein bar (116 kcal), and dinner of wholegrain pasta with spinach and chicken (504 kcal). Macros were protein 103g, carbs 71g, fat 25g, producing a caloric deficit of 2,100 kcal. Body weight recorded at 83.9 kg. Mood rating 6.7/10.
anchors: The scale
The walk along the N11
Jill in the car
Wine and two glasses
days.vibe:
Renate’s voice wakes me. She and Aimée are going to Alphen aan den Rijn for Aimée’s handball match. The shutter goes up and I get out of bed and walk to the bathroom. The scale is on the floor. I step on it and look down at the number on the display. It is lower than before. I step off and move it to another place in the bathroom. I step on it again. The number stays the same. I step off and into the shower.

I go to wake Jill to come along, but she stays in bed.

I get into the car and drive to the match. Renate is behind the canteen counter. It is half-time, people standing along the side watching, another group near the padel courts watching a different game. The game stays close and then it is over.

When I get home Jill is up and downstairs and we sit at the table eating. Renate gets a message from the mother of Jill’s best friend — does Jill want to come and play in Kerk en Zanen. I take her and park further along the N11 and start walking. The road runs parallel the whole way out towards Spookverlaat and Koudekerk aan den Rijn. I loop back to the car.

I go to pick Jill up after dinner and in the car she talks about her afternoon — that they cycled to Alphen for an ice cream, played outside, that the food was good. It comes in pieces.

At home I take a bottle of wine and put two glasses down. I take Dex out, switch everything off downstairs and go to bed.
days.personalInsights:
Day 119 closes Week 17 at 83.9 kg — a weight that holds significance not just as a number but as confirmation of a sustained directional trend. The scale ritual this morning — stepping off, repositioning, stepping on again — reflects something that has been present throughout this project: the instinct to verify, to not trust a single reading. The number held both times. That double-check is its own kind of data point.

The autonomic picture for the day is mixed but coherent. Morning RHR at 84 bpm sits at the higher end of the week’s baseline range, and waking HRV at 15 ms is suppressed — both consistent with a Sunday following a week of accumulated load. The recovery delta of +6 bpm is modest and acceptable. Sleep was close to six hours with adequate deep and REM stages, though restfulness at 76.7% suggests the body was working through the night rather than fully discharging. The 19 ms sleep HRV confirms this — functional but not restored.

The walk — nearly thirteen kilometres along the N11, looping back alone on a Sunday while the family was elsewhere — produced 821 active calories and pushed total daily burn to over three thousand. Against an intake of 960 kcal the deficit was substantial, the largest single-day deficit in recent weeks. Protein at 103g held well above target. What is notable is that the output came not from structured intensity but from duration and steadiness — 87% of training time in fat burn, average intensity at 71%. This is the pattern that has been building across Week 17: the body moving efficiently at moderate load, sustained over time, producing results without requiring peak effort. Twenty thousand steps on a day that began slowly, with one member of the family staying in bed and the morning organised around someone else’s schedule, is a quiet demonstration of how embedded this movement has become.

dailyDrift.interpretation: The evening-to-morning drift averaged +1.3 bpm across the week — up from Week 16's +0.6 bpm average. The average conceals sharp internal variation: Days 113 and 117 recorded genuine recovery (−10 and −8 bpm respectively), while Days 114–116 and 119 all showed positive drift (+5 to +15 bpm). Day 114's +15 bpm is the largest positive drift recorded since the early project. The week did not hold a consistent recovery shape — it alternated between genuine settling and days where accumulated load remained unresolved through the evening.

morningRHR.interpretation: The morning baseline averaged 81.1 bpm across the week — 0.5 bpm above Week 16's 80.6 bpm. The week was not uniform: Day 113 opened at 84 bpm (residual load from the Week 16 close), then dropped to 79 bpm for three consecutive days (114–116) — the lowest morning baseline cluster recorded since Week 15 — before returning to 84 bpm across the final two days (118–119). The mid-week stabilisation at 79 is meaningful. The return to 84 by Thursday and Friday suggests accumulating load as the week progressed rather than a sustained improvement.

sleepHRV.interpretation: Overnight HRV averaged 17.4 ms — a meaningful drop from Week 16's 22.9 ms average (−5.5 ms). The range across the week was 8–24 ms. Days 116, 117, and 118 form a consecutive declining sequence (15, 13, 8 ms) — the lowest values recorded in recent weeks, with Day 118 at 8 ms particularly notable. The week opened reasonably (Days 113–115: 21–24 ms), declined sharply through the second half, then partially recovered on Day 119 (19 ms). The system carried more autonomic stress in the second half of the week than the first.

weeklyTrendAnalysis.recommendation: Week 18 opens with a system that is somewhat depleted. The priority signal from Week 17 is recovery quality, not output volume. If sleep can be protected — 7.5h+ with meaningful deep sleep — the system has shown (Days 113–115) that it can recover quickly. Large deficits on short sleep days should be avoided. Training can continue but should carry more fat-burn zone work and less cumulative volume early in the week, allowing the HRV floor to rise before adding load. Week 18's autonomic picture will be defined by what the first two or three days allow to recover.
weeklyTrendAnalysis.overallTrend: Week 17 was a week of high output and progressive autonomic depletion. Training volume was substantial across all seven days — distances in the 10–16 km range, back-to-back long sessions early in the week, caloric deficits consistently large across multiple days. The body absorbed this load reasonably through mid-week, then showed clear strain signals from Day 116 onward: HRV falling, morning baselines rising, positive drift becoming the norm. Sunday's weight reading of 83.9 kg confirms the physical output translated — that figure sits within or below the Week 17 target band (84.2–84.8 kg), meaning the week's sustained deficit and training volume produced a measurable result. The week closed at a lower autonomic level than it opened, but the weight reading indicates the depletion was productive rather than incidental.

days.dailySummary: Day 78 opened Week 12 with a long walk — 2h 49m 57s covering 16.36 km at 68% average intensity, 92% fat-burn zone dominance, 955 active calories. Sleep was solid: 8h 5m at 84.2% restfulness with ideal architecture (0 min awake, 1h 25m REM, 5h 38m Core, 1h 2m Deep). Sleep HRV 22ms, waking HRV 29ms showing healthy autonomic activation. Morning resting HR baseline 79 bpm (dominant stable cluster from raw readings), waking bpm 72, evening resting 84 bpm (+5 bpm recovery indicator reflecting training load). Total daily activity: 23,025 steps, 18.90 km, 181 exercise minutes, 11/11 stand hours. Nutrition: 1,525 kcal intake (53% carbs, 180% protein, 115% fat), 2,929 kcal burned, 1,404 kcal deficit. Fasting 18:6 maintained. Mood 6.7/10 — grounded and forward-facing after a discouraging Week 11 weigh-in. Good weather supported a long walk with Dex. 78 consecutive days without exception.
days.vibe:
Week 11 ended on the scale going the wrong way, and that discouragement was real. Nothing managed it away. The numbers didn't cooperate, and you sat with that honestly. That takes something — the willingness to feel what the data says without letting it define what comes next.

But Monday arrived anyway, and so did you.

The weather was good and Dex was ready. You went out together without ceremony, without a speech about fresh starts or new resolutions. Just a quiet decision that the week still deserved showing up for — that the process was worth trusting even when the scale wasn't cooperating. The walk stretched long. Two hours and forty-nine minutes of movement with the fat-burn zone holding steady almost the entire time. Not pushed. Not forced. Just the body doing what it's learned to do over seventy-eight consecutive days: move efficiently, sustain without drama, come home tired in the good way.

There's a particular kind of evidence that lives in the biometrics, separate from weight. Your sleep the night before was near-perfect — eight hours and five minutes, zero minutes awake, deep sleep present, architecture intact. Your nervous system didn't carry the week's discouragement into the night. It recovered anyway. That gap between how something feels and what the body actually does — that gap is where seventy-eight days of discipline quietly live.

The morning resting heart rate held at 79. The waking bpm was 72 — calm, like a body that's been here before and knows what Monday asks of it. The evening came in at 84, a gentle climb that reflects the load rather than the stress. The deficit crossed fourteen hundred calories. The steps crossed twenty-three thousand. The stand rings completed all eleven.

Week 12 begins. The target is the same as last week — the same because Week 11 didn't deliver what it was supposed to, not because anything broke. The target isn't a punishment. It's still the direction. The process is still intact. The hope is honest — that this week the scale reflects what the body has already been doing. Not certainty. Not optimism on command. Just the next step, chosen again.
days.personalInsights: Day 78 is the first day of Week 12, and it begins with something important: the choice to move after disappointment rather than collapse into it. The Week 11 weigh-in was discouraging, and that's acknowledged without minimization. What matters physiologically is that the body didn't reflect that discouragement — sleep architecture was near-perfect (0 min awake, strong deep and REM stages), morning RHR held at 79 bpm, and the training session covered 16.36 km in the fat-burn zone without redlining. The +5 bpm recovery indicator (79 morning → 84 evening) is proportional to the training load — a body that worked hard and is now recovering. The 1,404 kcal deficit is clean and substantial. The process is intact. Week 12 starts from the same foundation Week 11 was built on. The scale will follow when it follows. The discipline is already there.

days.dailySummary: Day 79 marked the return to volunteer work as an apprentice car mechanic at De Sleutelbloem in Noorden — the first day back after two months of Spravato treatment. Training: 1h 54m 41s walk covering 11.05 km at 68% average intensity, 95% fat-burn zone dominance (109 min), 639 active calories, avg HR 121 bpm (range 101–132). Sleep: 7h 23m at 83.0% restfulness — 1 min awake, 1h 40m REM, 4h 13m Core, 1h 30m Deep. Sleep HRV 14ms, waking HRV 18ms. Morning RHR baseline 79 bpm (lowest stable cluster, raw Apple Health data), waking bpm 84, evening resting HR 79 bpm — recovery indicator 0 bpm, reflecting complete autonomic restoration despite training load. Fasting 18:6 maintained. Mood 6.6/10. 79 consecutive days without exception.
days.vibe:
Two months is a long time to be inside a treatment protocol. Long enough that the outside world starts to feel slightly theoretical — something that exists and continues, but without you in it. De Sleutelbloem was still there. The garage smell was the same. The work was the same kind of quiet, tactile, grounded thing it always was.

Before taking the car to Zegveld for its MOT, there were brake pads to change. Hands on something real, something mechanical, something that needed doing and could be done. That kind of work has its own logic — you find the problem, you fix it, you move on. It doesn't ask how you're feeling. It just asks if you can do it. Today, you could.

Then the drive to Zegveld. Not a dramatic task. It doesn't announce itself. But there's something in the ordinariness of it that mattered today. You were trusted with someone's car. You had a destination. You had a small, real thing to do in the world, and you did it.

The walk happened too. The body has been doing this long enough now that it doesn't require a decision every few minutes — it just moves. That kind of repetition leaves something behind. Not momentum exactly. More like a settled expectation. Of course the walk happens. Of course the fasting holds. Of course the stand rings close.

Coming back to something you loved before the hard years took hold of things — that's its own kind of data point. Not measurable, but real.
days.personalInsights:
Day 79 carries two distinct signals worth tracking longitudinally. The first is physiological: a recovery indicator of 0 bpm (morning 79, evening 79) represents complete autonomic restoration after a nearly two-hour training session — the best possible outcome of the evening-minus-morning recovery metric. This continues the cardiovascular adaptation trend visible across Weeks 11 and 12, with morning RHR holding at 79 bpm for consecutive days and evening HR returning to baseline rather than elevating above it. Sleep architecture was strong: 1h 30m deep sleep is notably high, and only 1 minute of awake time reflects excellent sleep continuity. The 1,438 kcal deficit is clean and above the weekly average.

The second signal is contextual but clinically relevant: returning to volunteer work at De Sleutelbloem after two months represents a meaningful reintegration milestone. The Spravato treatment period required sustained withdrawal from routine social and functional activity. Re-engaging with purposeful, task-based work — even in a quiet, low-demand format — is a positive indicator of functional recovery alongside the physical metrics. Mood at 6.6/10 is consistent with recent baseline and appropriate given the mixed emotional texture of a first day back. The process remains intact on both dimensions.

days.vibe:
There is a steadiness that has no particular name. Not confidence, not momentum — something quieter than both. The body moved today the way it has learned to move: without requiring a reason each time, without needing the conditions to be right first. The walk happened. The distance accumulated. The hours passed.

The food was simple and sufficient. The fast held without effort. These things that once required willpower now just happen — not because the difficulty disappeared, but because the habit has gone deeper than the decision. Somewhere along the way, the choice became the default. The work became the ordinary shape of the day.

Eighty days is a long time to do the same thing. Long enough that the thing stops feeling like a commitment and starts feeling like identity. Not who you're trying to become. Who you already are. The body carries that now. The data reflects it. And somewhere beneath both, something quieter has settled in — the knowledge that showing up is no longer in question. Only the day itself remains.

The scale hasn't moved the way the work deserves. That discouragement is real and it hasn't fully lifted. But the body kept going anyway. Not despite the frustration — through it. That's the part that can't be faked, and the part that compounds.

days.vibe:
The weather made the decision before you did. That kind of morning doesn't ask permission — the light is right, the air has that particular quality, and your legs already know. Nieuwveen to Leiderdorp isn't a short walk. It's the kind of distance that asks something real of you, not as a test but as an invitation. You accepted.

Somewhere along the route, the watch died. The data stopped. The walk didn't. You kept moving through the gap where the numbers used to be, navigating by feel and familiarity rather than metrics. And when you arrived, you calculated the distance yourself — not because the number needed to exist, but because precision has become part of how you respect the work. Seventeen kilometers. You knew it before you confirmed it.

Your wife was waiting at Leiderdorp. Coffee followed. The ordinary warmth of being collected by someone who knows what you've been doing out there — not just today, but for eighty-one days. That moment at the café table, after the distance, after the cold air and the dead watch and the long route home — that's not a footnote to the training. That's the point of it. The body strong enough to walk that far. The life worth returning to at the end.

The watch going dark mid-route is its own kind of data. The walk continued without measurement because the habit is deeper than the tool now. You don't need the numbers to keep moving. You needed them once, to build trust in the process. Now the process runs on its own. The numbers are how you report what happened — not how you decide whether to go.

days.vibe:
Treatment days have their own particular rhythm. There is no surprise in the tiredness anymore — it is expected, even accepted — but expected does not mean easy. The body becomes heavier after, slower, quieter. The mind follows. And somewhere in that heaviness there is still the knowledge that this is the price, and that the price is worth paying. That knowing does not lift the tiredness, but it changes the relationship to it.

What makes these days bearable — more than bearable, actually meaningful — is that the trend is visible. Not loudly, not in ways that transform a morning, but in ways that accumulate quietly when looked at honestly. There is something happening. Something gradual and real. Each treatment carries a cost, and each cost is also a deposit into something that is slowly, unmistakably growing.

There was a small win today that had nothing to do with the body. Something technical that had been resisting for a week — an automation, a small system built to make things easier — finally worked. It is a minor thing in the scale of everything else, but minor things matter on heavy days. The satisfaction of persistence paying off in a quiet corner of the day was its own kind of medicine. Not everything that builds resilience announces itself.

The rest was chosen, the fast was kept, and the day was met. On a treatment Friday, that is enough.

days.vibe:
This morning my wife and I walked through Alphen aan den Rijn. Unhurried — the kind of pace you only find when there's nowhere urgent to be. We ended up at the Stadsherberg for coffee, sitting with it, not rushing. I've been doing this long enough now to recognize when something feels like recovery that isn't tracked anywhere. This morning was that. The body moving, but gently. The mind not racing. Just the two of us, and the town, and the morning doing its thing around us.

In the evening we went out for dinner with the kids. IS MORUS — Italian. It had been a long time since we'd done something like this as a family. My depression makes it hard. Not in a way that's always visible from the outside, but it's there — a kind of weight that makes ordinary things feel like they require more than they should. Planning, coordinating, getting everyone out the door, being present once you're there — it all costs something. And for a long time, the cost felt too high.

But tonight it didn't. Tonight I wanted to be there. The kids were there, my wife was there, and somewhere between the Aperol Spritz and the pizza I noticed I wasn't counting down to leaving. I was just in it. That's not a small thing for me. I know that. I try to hold it carefully when it happens, because I also know these evenings don't always go this way.

I try to savor these moments. I'm not always sure I succeed — the mind has a way of moving on before the heart is finished. But tonight I was there. And so were they. And the pizza was good. And that felt like enough.
days.personalInsights:
Day 83 sits at an interesting intersection: a physically active day — 15,645 steps, nearly two hours of training, 12.77 km total distance — combined with a socially rich one. The recovery delta of zero (evening RHR matching morning baseline at 84 bpm) suggests the body absorbed the day's load without autonomic strain, which is meaningful given the emotional weight social outings can carry for someone managing depression.

Sleep quality continues to show a pattern of generous duration but moderate restfulness — 9h 45min with 62.7% rating. The deep sleep figure of 1h 40min is solid. The 18:6 fast with a 1,208 kcal intake against 2,597 burned produces one of the larger deficits of recent weeks at 1,389 kcal, sustained without distress. The mood rating of 6.7 — above the recent midpoint — aligns with a day where the effort to show up was made and rewarded.

days.vibe:
It's been another week where the scale hasn't moved, and I really don't know what I'm doing wrong. I've walked, I've fasted, I've shown up every single day — and the number just sits there. It doesn't feel good. After all this effort, after everything I've put into this, I expected something. Some sign that the body is listening. And instead: nothing. My motivation is taking a hit right now. I won't pretend otherwise.

But the morning was something different. I first took Aimée to Alphen aan den Rijn, then went back to pick up Renate and Jill so we could all be there together — Aimée's first handball tournament. They won. And Aimée scored. I don't know how to describe what that feels like as a dad. You just want to be there for those moments, and today I was. We all were.

When we got home, Renate and I cleaned the garden together. One of those quiet, productive things you do side by side without needing to say much. And later I went for a walk. Not a training walk — just a walk. The kind where you're not counting anything, you're just moving through the day.

Two things can be true at the same time. I can be frustrated with the weight and still have had a good Sunday. I'm trying to hold both without letting one cancel out the other. It doesn't always work. But today, mostly, it did.
days.personalInsights:
Day 84 closes Week 12 with a mood rating of 5.7 — the lowest of the week — driven not by any physical failure but by a psychological one: another weigh-in at 87 kg, unchanged despite consistent fasting, elevated step counts, and maintained deficits. The data contradicts the plateau. A 1,431 kcal deficit on a day with 12,399 steps and a 395-calorie training session reflects genuine energetic output. The pattern across the week suggests the body may be in a compensation phase — water retention, hormonal adaptation, or measurement timing — rather than true stagnation.

The sleep architecture is solid: 8h 24m with 1h 14m deep and 1h 47m REM, restfulness at 68.1%. The evening HR dropping 5 bpm below the morning baseline is a positive autonomic signal — the day's physical and emotional load was absorbed without residual strain. The training, while shorter in distance (7.72 km vs recent highs), maintained 80% fat-burn zone dominance.

The real story of Day 84 is the tension between longitudinal frustration and present-moment reward. Aimée scoring at her first handball tournament, the garden work with Renate, an evening walk — these are the days the project is built around, even when the metrics don't cooperate. Motivation fluctuates. Twelve weeks of consistency is the counter-argument to a single number on a scale.

dailyDrift.interpretation: Evening HR finished exactly at morning resting level on average across the week. Neither deteriorating nor recovering — the recovery system is in equilibrium, managing load without falling behind.

weeklyTrendAnalysis.recommendation: Monitor morning RHR closely in Week 13. If it remains above 82 bpm, consider one active recovery day. Sleep HRV stability is a positive sign — maintain current fasting and nutrition protocol. The weight plateau is psychological terrain now as much as physical.
weeklyTrendAnalysis.overallTrend: Week 12 recovery data shows a system under load but holding. Morning RHR edged upward while HRV remained stable — a pattern consistent with accumulated training stress rather than overtraining. The neutral daily drift signals equilibrium. Physiologically, the body is doing the work even as the scale stays flat.
